title: Coffee (Black) on Cotton
stainChemistry: Tannins are polyphenolic plant compounds (from tea, coffee, red wine, fruit juices) that bond to fibers through hydrogen bonding and π-stacking interactions with cellulose and protein fibers. Fresh tannin stains are removable with mild detergent. Aged stains oxidize and darken: the brown color is caused by oxidation products. Hydrogen peroxide 6% breaks the double bonds in the oxidized tannin molecules, reversing the browning. Sodium hydrosulfite provides a reducing alternative for fibers that cannot tolerate bleaching. Black coffee is a pure tannin stain with no fat or protein components.
whyThisWorks: Coffee gets its dark color from plant pigments (chlorogenic acids, tannins) in roasted beans that bond to cotton cellulose through hydrogen bonding. Cotton absorbs liquids quickly, pulling the stain deep into the weave. Tannin Formula (acidic) breaks the initial tannin-fiber bonds. H₂O₂ 6% cleaves the double bonds in oxidized tannin molecules, turning the stain colorless. NSD and acetic acid flush and restore the fiber. Cotton tolerates peroxide well.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Apply Tannin Formula (acidic tannin spotter) and tamp gently. Dwell 5–10 minutes. This acidic spotter specifically targets the bonds that coffee, wine, tea, and fruit juice pigments form with fabric. Work from the edge toward the center.

2. Apply H₂O₂ 6% (professional-grade oxidizer) to the stain. Dwell 5–15 minutes, checking every 5 minutes. This oxidizer breaks down the double bonds that make tannin stains brown. On colored fabrics, test an inconspicuous area first.

3. Flush with NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) with a mild leveling agent. Dwell 2–3 minutes. Removes broken-down tannin pigment and excess oxidizer. The leveling agent helps relax the fiber so trapped pigment can release. Rinse thoroughly.
4. Apply 28% Acetic acid (diluted 1:10) to stop any remaining oxidizer activity and restore the fabric to its natural state. Dwell 1–2 minutes. Blot dry and allow to air dry completely.
products: NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) with leveling agent
professionalProtocol.temperature: ambient — never use hot water on tannin stains. Heat accelerates tannin oxidation and polymerization, locking the brown color into the fiber.
warnings: Using hot water — accelerates oxidation and sets the tannin color faster.
Applying hydrogen peroxide to colored fabrics without testing — peroxide bleaches fabric color along with the stain.
Oversaturation with peroxide — can cause yellowing on whites from oxidized cellulose.
Not rinsing peroxide completely — residual peroxide continues bleaching and weakens fiber.
NEVER use ammonia, baking soda, or alkaline agents — permanently darkens tannin stains.

steps: 1. Blot the stain with a clean white cloth to soak up as much as you can.
2. Mix 1 tbsp Dawn dish soap with 1 cup 3% hydrogen peroxide in a bowl. Apply the solution and let it sit for 5–10 minutes.
3. Blot with a clean white cloth. Repeat until the color fades.
4. Rinse with cold water to remove all cleaning solution.
5. Air dry — don't use a dryer until the stain is gone. Heat from a dryer can permanently set any remaining stain.
products: OxiClean Versatile Stain Remover (for set stains)
White vinegar (separate spray bottle for stubborn stains)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: For set-in tannin stains, mix baking soda with a little hydrogen peroxide to make a paste, apply for 15 minutes, then rinse. If the stain survives two rounds of treatment, a professional cleaner can use concentrated agents and specialized equipment that outperform any home method.
warnings: Test any cleaning solution on a hidden seam or inside hem first.
Check for colorfastness before using hydrogen peroxide.
Avoid heat until the stain is completely gone.
Speed matters — fresh tannin stains lift much more easily than dried ones.
NEVER use ammonia, baking soda, or alkaline products — permanently darkens tannin stains.

neverDo: Never use hot water — sets tannin color permanently.
Never use ammonia, baking soda, or alkaline agents — permanently darkens tannin stains.
Never mix ammonia with chlorine bleach — produces toxic chloramine gas.
Never put in the dryer before stain is fully gone.
fiberSensitivities: Cotton (cellulose) tolerates most agents including dilute bleach on whites.
Colored cotton: test peroxide in a hidden area first.
Heavy peroxide exposure on cotton can cause yellowing from oxidized cellulose.

escalation.when: If peroxide bleaching reaches a plateau, the tannin has likely cross-linked with the fiber polymer. Try sequential treating: alternate between peroxide and leveling agent. For extreme cases, recommend professional-grade tannin-specific chemistry.
escalation.whatToTell: We've treated the coffee on your garment. Coffee darkens with air exposure and sets deeper the longer it sits.

contextQuestions: How old is the stain — was it treated immediately or has it been sitting?
Is this black coffee or coffee with cream/sugar? (cream and sugar change the protocol)
Has the garment been through a dryer since the stain occurred?
Is the cotton light/white or heavily dyed?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Black coffee is a pure tannin stain — straightforward protocol. Coffee with cream adds a fat component requiring POG or NSD pre-treatment. Coffee with sugar adds a caramelized sugar component. A heat-set stain requires longer Tannin Formula dwell and multiple peroxide passes. A garment run through the dryer has significantly reduced recovery odds.
